Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause; subject (Yvy) + intransitive verb (oikove) + locative complement (nde pype)
Figurative Meaning: To be deeply connected to one’s land or identity
Usage Contexts: Cultural identity, heritage discussions, community gatherings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Emerillon cosmology where land and personhood are spiritually intertwined
First Example Sentence: Ale he’i: yvy oikove nde pype, ani reheja.
Second Example Sentence: Ore ru omombe’u ore rehe: yvy oikove nde pype.
Third Example Sentence: Oko oikuaa yvy oikove nde pype araka’eve.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial clause; noun phrase + transitive verb + object
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: Ere nde py’a guasu ha ere mbarete
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional moral teaching transmitted orally in village councils
First Example Sentence: Ore sy he’i: py’a guasu ogueru mbarete.
Second Example Sentence: Ale oikotevẽ py’a guasu ogueru mbarete ko ára.
Third Example Sentence: Oko omano’ẽ py’a guasu ogueru mbarete.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative; noun + negated verb + adverb
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from agricultural life along the Oyapock River
First Example Sentence: Ore jarýi he’i ka’a ndopytai rei.
Second Example Sentence: Ale oikuaa ka’a ndopytai rei tembiapo rehe.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Metaphor from river flow in the Amazonian basin
First Example Sentence: Ore ru he’i ysyry oguata mbegue.
Second Example Sentence: Ale oikuaa ysyry oguata mbegue tembiapo rehe.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative reflexive clause; repetition for emphasis
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used historically in conflict storytelling traditions
First Example Sentence: Oko he’i tata ndohesarái tatáre politika rehe.
Third Example Sentence: Ore ruvicha omombe’u tata ndohesarái tatáre.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simple declarative; animate subject + verb + object
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Bird imagery common in Emerillon storytelling
Second Example Sentence: Oko omombe’u guyra oikuaa yvága mbo’epy rehe.
Third Example Sentence: Ore sy omyesakã guyra oikuaa yvága.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects communal ethics and compassion norms
First Example Sentence: Ore jarýi he’i maino’ẽ oikove py’a rupi.
Second Example Sentence: Ale ohecha maino’ẽ oikove py’a rupi nde rehe.
Third Example Sentence: Oko oñandú maino’ẽ oikove py’a rupi.

Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: Ohasa asy ha oikuaa mba’éichapa oiko
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Forest-based imagery tied to Maroni region
Third Example Sentence: Ore ru omombe’u yvyra oikuaa yvytu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative; animate noun + verb + temporal object
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used in storytelling about justice and reconciliation
Third Example Sentence: Ore ruvicha oñe’ẽ eirete oikuaa ára.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simple declarative; temporal noun + verb + object
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Morning symbolism central in village routines
First Example Sentence: Ore sy he’i ko’ẽ ogueru tesape.
Second Example Sentence: Ale omombe’u ko’ẽ ogueru tesape ko’ẽrõ.
